title: AI, De-Mystified
thesis: AI terminology becomes more useful when each term is explained through plain language, older related concepts, practical examples, limitations, and academic roots instead of being treated as a fresh breakthrough every time.
primary_reader: A curious builder, student, creator, or knowledge worker who uses AI tools and wants to understand new AI terms without memorizing jargon or buying into hype.
intended_outcome: The reader can understand one AI concept at a time, compare it with ideas they already know, explain where it helps in practice, and identify what part of the term is real versus inflated.
artifact_fit: A series of short articles fits better than a single guide because the learning goal is recurring literacy: each term deserves its own analogy, practical examples, anti-hype check, and academic bridge.
smallest_viable_version: A series charter plus a first article on loops versus goals, using a repeatable article template and a light source seed for deeper research.
scope_risks: The series could become a broad AI encyclopedia, a product comparison hub, or an academic survey. It should stay focused on one concept at a time with practical examples and clear boundaries.
alternatives_considered: A separate website was considered, but Aura Knowledge is the better first home because the series is about knowledge literacy and durable concept-building. A microsite can follow later if the series gains its own audience.
claims: AI buzzwords become easier to evaluate when each term is compared with older concepts rather than introduced as an isolated novelty.
Long-running AI sessions are best understood as goal-directed systems that use loops, context, memory, and evaluation rather than as indefinite conversations.
A recurring anti-hype section can help readers separate practical value from branding without making the series cynical.
The first article should cover loops versus goals because that distinction clarifies later topics such as agents, memory, context management, tool use, and evaluations.
Short, repeatable articles are a better teaching format for this topic than one large guide because readers can learn and revisit one concept at a time.

sources.title: Sutton and Barto: Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction
ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

sanitized_summary: This proposal defines an Aura Knowledge series that explains modern AI terms one at a time using plain language, relevant analogies, practical examples, benefits, limitations, future use cases, and academic connections. The recommended first article is about loops versus goals in long-running AI sessions.

abstraction_examples.original: I want to learn these AI buzzwords regularly and later share the articles with others.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: The series serves readers who want a reusable, public learning path for understanding AI terminology one concept at a time.

abstraction_examples.original: People keep creating a facade around AI terminology and I want to clarify it.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: The series includes a recurring anti-hype check that separates practical value from branding or unnecessary complexity.

abstraction_examples.original: I am excited to learn briefly about these topics and then jump into details.
abstraction_examples.abstracted: Each article starts with a brief explanation, then offers optional deeper sections for academic connections and advanced use cases.

agent_involvement: Drafted with AI agent assistance using the Aura article-proposal ideation workflow; human author retains final judgment.

publication.note: Series guide and first article (Loops vs Goals) published in Aura Knowledge.
